critic_raw:
## Analysis

This is a well-structured experiment with genuine attention to leakage and temporal validity. I'll focus on the issues I consider substantive enough to undermine the hypothesis test.

---

### Issue 1: Preprocessing Normalization Uses Full 18-Month Corpus (Label and Distribution Leakage Forward in Time)

**Claim:** Fitting preprocessing transformers (scaling, encoding, normalization, imputation defaults) on the full 18-month corpus before model fitting is presented as a stabilization technique that avoids month-to-month distribution instability.

**Why this is wrong:** This is a form of future data leakage. When normalizing, say, inter-packet delay distributions, the scaler's parameters are informed by data from months 14–18 — data the model is not supposed to have seen. For heavy-tailed features with temporal drift (which the authors explicitly acknowledge), the normalization parameters will systematically shift all time windows toward a distribution that reflects the future. The effect is most pronounced for sparse categorical encodings (hashed user agents, port categories) where vocabulary coverage and frequency statistics at evaluation time are silently baked into the encoding fitted on future data. This doesn't just bias evaluation upward — it specifically inflates performance on distribution-shifted test data, which is precisely the regime the experiment is designed to stress-test. The transformer's learnable embeddings are especially exposed here, since embedding lookup tables initialized or regularized against full-corpus frequencies will receive implicit priors that could not be known at training time.

**Evidence:** Refit all preprocessing on training data only (months 1–10), apply frozen to validation and test. Compare pAUC-ROC on the test set and per-month breakdown. If the gap narrows or the temporal degradation curve changes shape, the normalization leakage was load-bearing. The effect can also be isolated by checking whether test-set normalization residuals are systematically smaller than they would be under a training-only fit.

---

### Issue 2: The Identity Leakage Control Is Asymmetric and May Not Isolate the Causal Mechanism

**Claim:** Training a second transformer without host identifiers and comparing to the baseline controls for identity-based shortcuts. If performance degrades significantly, leakage drives the advantage; if not, sequence modeling does.

**Why this is wrong:** The control is structurally asymmetric. The baseline never had host identifiers — it was designed without them. The transformer-without-identifiers variant is still a transformer trained on packet sequences, session metadata, and all other features, including the "historical features" (e.g., alert count on source host in the past 7 days). Historical alert counts are a soft form of host identity — a high-entropy, temporally stable signal that correlates strongly with host identity even after hashing or removing explicit IPs. The transformer can memorize host behavior through historical features even when IPs are stripped. This means the ablation will not cleanly isolate sequence modeling versus identity shortcuts. More fundamentally, the experiment is trying to answer: "does packet-level sequence structure add value over aggregated statistics?" The cleanest test would be transformer-on-sequences-only versus baseline-on-aggregated-statistics-only, not the full model comparison with asymmetric feature sets.

**Evidence:** Train a third transformer variant using only the packet sequence features (no session metadata, no historical features) and compare to the baseline. If that variant still outperforms the baseline on test data from novel campaigns, the sequence structure hypothesis has real support. Separately, measure mutual information between hashed host identifiers and historical alert features to quantify the residual identity signal.

---

### Issue 3: Low-FPR pAUC Calibration on Validation Set Creates Threshold-to-Regime Coupling

**Claim:** The 5% FPR operating threshold is calibrated on the validation set and then applied to the test set for recall computation. pAUC over 0–5% FPR is stable across months with different incident prevalence.

**Why this is wrong (two related sub-issues):**

a) FPR is computed over the full session population. If incident prevalence changes between validation and test months — which is likely over a 5-month window with different attack campaigns — the same score threshold corresponds to different FPR values in each period. A threshold calibrated to 5% FPR in months 11–13 may correspond to 2% or 9% FPR in month 16 if the benign session volume changes. The authors note pAUC is more stable across prevalence, which is true for ranking metrics, but the secondary metric (recall at exactly 5% FPR) requires a threshold, and that threshold is prevalence-dependent.

b) pAUC is typically normalized by the width of the FPR range (0.05) to produce values in [0,1]. If the authors use unnormalized pAUC, comparisons across months with different class distributions will conflate ranking quality with volume. This is a silent misconfiguration risk: both implementations could look identical in code while one normalizes and the other does not.

**Evidence:** Report per-month score distributions and check whether the validation-calibrated threshold produces consistent FPR on each test month separately. For the pAUC computation, confirm normalization in the code and report both normalized and unnormalized values. If per-month FPR drifts substantially from 5%, the recall-at-threshold secondary metric is not measuring what it claims.

---

### Issue 4: 5-Fold Temporal Cross-Validation Within a 3-Month Validation Window Is Structurally Degenerate

**Claim:** 5-fold temporal cross-validation within months 11–13 provides reliable hyperparameter selection without data leakage.

**Why this is wrong:** A 3-month window divided into 5 sequential folds yields folds of approximately 18 days each. The training sets for early folds are extremely short (as few as 18 days in fold 1), and the temporal autocorrelation of network traffic — weekly seasonality, recurring scanning behavior, persistent C2 callback patterns — means that adjacent folds are highly correlated. Specifically, fold k trains on folds 1..k-1 and evaluates on fold k. For fold 2, training data is 18 days and evaluation is 18 days. APT campaigns that span this window will appear in both, and the 24-hour session labeling window means a campaign active in training days 1–18 will produce correlated sessions in evaluation days 19–36. This isn't temporal leakage per se but it means variance estimates from this cross-validation are severely underestimated — the folds are not exchangeable. Hyperparameters selected on this process may overfit to the specific campaigns active in months 11–13.

**Evidence:** Compare hyperparameters selected by this 5-fold procedure against a simpler strategy: train on months 11–12, validate on month 13. If the selected hyperparameters differ substantially, the cross-validation is selecting on noise. More diagnostically, check whether the cross-validation performance estimate matches test-set performance — large gaps indicate the CV procedure is miscalibrated.

---

### Summary of Root Causes

Issues 1 and 4 share a root cause: the temporal isolation principle stated in Section 3 is violated in the implementation details (preprocessing and CV). Issues 2 and 3 share a root cause: the confound controls are asymmetric in ways that make it impossible to cleanly attribute any observed advantage to the hypothesized mechanism (packet-level sequence modeling).

Issue 1 (preprocessing leakage on the full corpus) is the most structurally damaging because it specifically inflates performance in the distribution-shifted regime, making the transformer look more robust to adversary adaptation than it actually is — which is the central claim of the hypothesis.
defender_raw:
## Pass 1 — Analysis of Each Critique Point

---

### Critique 1: Preprocessing normalization fitted on the full 18-month corpus constitutes future data leakage

**Critic's claim:** Fitting scalers, encoders, and imputation defaults on the full 18-month corpus (including test months 14–18) leaks future distributional information into training and validation preprocessing, specifically inflating performance on the distribution-shifted test regime.

**Assessment:** This critique is valid and I cannot adequately rebut it. The design intent was to achieve stable normalization parameters for heavy-tailed and sparse-categorical features — a reasonable engineering goal — but the execution is wrong. Fitting on the full corpus violates the temporal isolation principle stated in Section 3. The effect is not hypothetical: for heavy-tailed flow features, the scaler's mean and variance will be shifted by extreme values that occur only in future months; for sparse categoricals (hashed user agents, port categories, DNS domains), vocabulary coverage and frequency priors will reflect attack infrastructure and user behavior that does not exist in the training window. The critic's point that this specifically inflates robustness to distribution shift is well-taken — the experiment is explicitly designed to stress-test temporal generalization, so this is precisely the wrong dimension to contaminate.

The transformer's learnable embedding tables are especially exposed: if embedding initialization or regularization is informed (even implicitly through preprocessing statistics) by the full corpus, the model receives a prior over token frequencies that could not have been known at deployment time.

**Concession:** This is a genuine flaw. The correct procedure is to fit all preprocessing transformers exclusively on training data (months 1–10), freeze those parameters, and apply them to validation and test without refitting. The critic's proposed diagnostic — compare per-month pAUC degradation curves under training-only normalization versus full-corpus normalization — is exactly the right test, and the result could materially change conclusions about temporal robustness.

**What remains genuinely uncertain:** The magnitude of the effect is unknown without running the corrected experiment. For well-behaved continuous features (session duration, byte counts), the bias may be small. For sparse categoricals with long tails and temporal novelty (new C2 domains, novel user agents in later months), it could be large. The critic is right that this is unknowable without empirical comparison.

---

### Critique 2: The identity leakage ablation is asymmetric — historical alert-count features are a soft host-identity signal

**Critic's claim:** The transformer-without-identifiers ablation still includes historical alert-count features (e.g., alert count on source host in the past 7 days), which are a temporally stable, high-entropy proxy for host identity. This means the ablation does not cleanly isolate sequence modeling from identity shortcuts. The cleanest test would be sequences-only transformer versus statistics-only baseline.

**Assessment:** The critic is substantially correct. Historical alert-count features are computed per-host and are strong proxies for host identity — a host that has been flagged repeatedly in the prior 7 days is effectively identified by that feature even without an explicit IP. The transformer can learn to recognize specific hosts through this channel. The ablation as designed does not answer the question it claims to answer: it compares "transformer with sequences and metadata minus explicit IPs" against a baseline that never had IPs or historical alert features in the same way. The causal attribution — "advantage comes from sequence modeling, not identity" — is not cleanly supported.

**Partial rebuttal:** The design intent of the ablation was narrower than the critic's framing suggests: it was designed to test whether explicit IP/hostname inclusion drives the advantage, not to fully isolate sequence modeling. That is a defensible narrower question, and the methodology document does acknowledge this asymmetry implicitly by calling for a "second transformer variant" rather than a full feature-matched ablation. However, the stated interpretation in Section 9 — "if performance persists when identifiers are removed, sequence modeling drives performance" — overclaims what the ablation can support. The critic is correct that this logical step fails.

**Concession:** The sequences-only transformer vs. statistics-only baseline comparison the critic proposes is necessary to support the stated hypothesis. Without it, the experiment cannot distinguish sequence modeling value from residual identity leakage through historical features. I concede this is a real gap in the confound control structure.

**What remains genuinely uncertain:** Whether historical alert features actually constitute a practically load-bearing identity signal depends on host population size and alert rate distributions. In large enterprise environments with thousands of hosts, the per-host alert history may not function as a strong identifier. This is empirically testable via the mutual information measurement the critic suggests.

---

### Critique 3: FPR-threshold drift across test months and pAUC normalization ambiguity

**Critic's claim (two sub-issues):** (a) A threshold calibrated to 5% FPR on validation months may correspond to materially different effective FPR on test months with different benign session volumes, making recall-at-5%-FPR a drifting target. (b) Normalized vs. unnormalized pAUC is an ambiguous implementation choice that creates a silent misconfiguration risk.

**Assessment of sub-issue (a):** The critic is correct in principle. FPR is a function of the score threshold and the volume of true negatives; if the benign session volume changes significantly across months (due to network growth, traffic pattern changes, or seasonal effects), a fixed score threshold will produce different effective FPRs. This is a known limitation of threshold-based secondary metrics. However, the primary metric — pAUC-ROC over 0–5% FPR — is a ranking metric and is by construction invariant to the score threshold choice; it evaluates all possible thresholds in that FPR range. The critic's concern applies specifically to the secondary metric (recall at exactly 5% FPR), not to the primary metric. The methodology correctly identifies pAUC as more stable, but the secondary metric has the problem the critic describes.

**Partial rebuttal:** The secondary metric is explicitly labeled secondary. If the per-month FPR drift is reported (as the methodology specifies in Section 8), analysts can assess whether the recall-at-threshold secondary metric is interpretable. The diagnostic is built into the design. This is a presentation and interpretation risk, not a fundamental flaw in the primary metric.

**Assessment of sub-issue (b):** The pAUC normalization ambiguity is a real silent misconfiguration risk. Unnormalized pAUC grows with the FPR interval width and cannot be compared across months with different effective FPR ranges; normalized pAUC (divided by FPR interval width, yielding values in [0,1]) is the appropriate form for cross-month comparison. The critic is right that two implementations can look identical in code while differing on this. This is a documentation and code-review issue, not a fundamental design flaw, but it is a genuine risk.

**Concession:** Sub-issue (a) is a real limitation of the secondary metric that the methodology does not fully acknowledge. Sub-issue (b) is a legitimate implementation risk that should be explicitly specified. Neither rises to the level of invalidating the primary metric or the overall experimental structure, but both represent gaps.

**What remains genuinely uncertain:** The magnitude of benign volume variation across the 5 test months is unknown without examining the data. If benign session counts are stable (±10%), threshold drift is minor. If they vary substantially (±50%), the secondary metric is unreliable.

---

### Critique 4: 5-fold temporal cross-validation within a 3-month validation window produces degenerate folds

**Critic's claim:** A 3-month window divided into 5 sequential folds yields ~18-day folds. APT campaigns and weekly network seasonality mean adjacent folds are highly correlated, variance estimates are underestimated, and hyperparameters may overfit to the specific campaigns in months 11–13.

**Assessment:** The critic is correct that 18-day folds are very short for a problem with weekly seasonality, persistent C2 patterns, and 24-hour labeling windows. The earliest fold (fold 1 trains on 18 days, evaluates on 18 days) provides an extremely small hyperparameter signal, and the autocorrelation concern is legitimate. However, I want to be precise about what this breaks: it makes the CV procedure's variance estimates unreliable, which affects confidence in hyperparameter selection — it does not introduce leakage in the strict sense, because the folds are still chronologically ordered. The risk is that selected hyperparameters are optimized for campaign-specific patterns in months 11–13 rather than generalizable structure.

**Partial rebuttal:** The final test on months 14–18 is independent of the CV procedure entirely. Even if hyperparameter selection is noisy or campaign-specific, the test-set pAUC-ROC provides an honest evaluation of the selected models. The critic's concern is that the CV procedure might have selected different hyperparameters under a better procedure, potentially yielding a different (better or worse) test result. This is a real limitation but it does not invalidate the test-set comparison — it only means the models may not be optimally tuned.

**Partial concession:** The critic's alternative — train on months 11–12, validate on month 13 — is simpler and more robust for this problem. The 5-fold structure inside 3 months is overcomplicated and may not add value. The concern about hyperparameter overfitting to months 11–13 campaigns is legitimate, particularly if those campaigns are not representative of months 14–18. I concede the CV design is suboptimal, though not fatally flawed.

**What remains genuinely uncertain:** Whether the hyperparameter sensitivity actually matters depends on how flat the hyperparameter landscape is for both models. If XGBoost and the transformer both perform similarly across a wide range of reasonable hyperparameter settings, this issue is immaterial. If the models are highly sensitive to specific choices, it could affect the comparative conclusion.

---

## Pass 2 — Verdict Selection

Summarizing the concessions:

1. **Issue 1 (preprocessing leakage):** Full concession. This is a genuine, structurally damaging flaw. It specifically inflates performance in the distribution-shifted regime — the central claim of the experiment. The magnitude is unknown without rerunning the corrected experiment.

2. **Issue 2 (asymmetric identity ablation):** Substantial concession. The stated causal attribution in Section 9 is not supported by the ablation as designed. A sequences-only vs. statistics-only comparison is necessary to support the hypothesis.

3. **Issue 3 (FPR drift and pAUC normalization):** Partial concession. The secondary metric has a real threshold-drift problem; the primary metric is sound. The normalization ambiguity is a documentation risk, not a design failure.

4. **Issue 4 (degenerate CV folds):** Partial concession. The CV procedure is suboptimal but does not invalidate the test-set evaluation.

Issues 1 and 2 are both critical-level flaws. Issue 1 means we cannot trust the reported temporal robustness results without rerunning with training-only preprocessing. Issue 2 means we cannot attribute any observed advantage to sequence modeling without the cleaner ablation. Neither can be resolved by argument alone — both require rerunning experiments with corrected protocols. The overall verdict is therefore `empirical_test_agreed`: the hypothesis may still be supportable, but the current experimental results cannot be trusted to answer it cleanly without the corrected implementations.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing normalization fitted on the full 18-month corpus (including test data) constitutes future data leakage, specifically inflating performance on the distribution-shifted test regime the experiment is designed to stress-test.

The identity leakage ablation is asymmetric: historical alert-count features provide a soft host-identity signal in the transformer-without-identifiers variant, so the ablation does not cleanly isolate sequence modeling from identity shortcuts.

FPR-threshold calibration on the validation set will produce drifting effective FPR on test months with different benign session volumes, and the pAUC normalization convention is ambiguous, creating a silent misconfiguration risk.

5-fold temporal cross-validation within a 3-month window produces degenerate folds (~18 days each) with high autocorrelation across adjacent folds, making variance estimates unreliable and hyperparameter selection potentially overfit to the specific campaigns in months 11–13.
all_issues_adjudicated: Preprocessing normalization fitted on the full 18-month corpus leaks future distributional information into test evaluation, specifically inflating apparent temporal robustness — the central claim of the experiment — and the magnitude of this effect cannot be determined without rerunning with training-only normalization.

The identity leakage ablation does not cleanly isolate sequence modeling from host-identity shortcuts because historical alert-count features remain in the transformer-without-identifiers variant, and the causal attribution in Section 9 therefore overclaims what the ablation supports.

The secondary recall-at-5%-FPR metric is subject to threshold drift across test months with different benign session volumes, and the pAUC normalization convention is underspecified, creating a silent misconfiguration risk for cross-month comparisons.

5-fold temporal cross-validation within a 3-month window produces ~18-day folds with high autocorrelation, making variance estimates unreliable and hyperparameter selection potentially overfit to the specific campaigns in months 11–13.
